learning_objectives: Explain why communication is the most critical PM skill
Calculate communication channels using the formula n(n-1)/2

lecture_notes.introduction: Project managers spend up to 90% of their time communicating. Poor communication is the leading cause of project failure.
key_concepts: Communication channels formula: n(n-1)/2 where n = number of stakeholders
Communication management plan: who needs what information, when, and how
Communication models: sender-encoder-medium-decoder-receiver
Communication types: formal/informal, written/verbal, internal/external
Push vs. pull vs. interactive communication
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating communication channels for a 15-person project team and designing a communication matrix

review.value: A team of 10 has 10(9)/2 = 45 channels; adding one person creates 10 new channels (55 total)
Push sends info to recipients (email); pull makes info available for retrieval (intranet); interactive requires real-time exchange (meetings)

learning_objectives: Write clear and concise project status reports
Structure professional email communications for project contexts
Create project dashboards that convey key information at a glance
readings: HBR Guide to Better Business Writing, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Written communication creates the project record. Clear writing prevents misunderstandings and protects the project team.
key_concepts: Status report structure: accomplishments, plans, issues, risks, metrics
Executive summary writing: bottom-line-up-front (BLUF) format
Email etiquette: clear subject lines, action items, appropriate recipients
Dashboard design: KPIs, RAG status (red/amber/green), trend indicators
Document version control and distribution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Transforming a poorly written status report into a clear, actionable one using BLUF format

review.value: State the conclusion or request first, then provide supporting details; respects the reader's time
Red = critical issue, Amber = at risk, Green = on track; provides instant visual assessment

learning_objectives: Plan and facilitate effective project meetings
Design agendas that drive decisions and actions

lecture_notes.introduction: Meetings are the primary interactive communication tool. Poorly run meetings waste time and money; well-run meetings drive progress.
key_concepts: Meeting types: status, decision, problem-solving, brainstorming, kickoff
Effective agenda: objectives, topics with time boxes, pre-work, decision items
Facilitation techniques: parking lot, round-robin, timeboxing, fist-of-five
Meeting minutes: decisions made, action items with owners and deadlines
Virtual meeting best practices: camera on, engagement techniques, recording
lecture_notes.demonstration: Facilitating a simulated project status meeting with role-playing common difficult scenarios

review.value: Captures off-topic but important items for later discussion without derailing the current meeting
Who will do what by when; every action item needs an owner and a deadline to be effective

learning_objectives: Tailor communication style and content to different stakeholder groups
Apply influence techniques without formal authority
Manage upward communication with sponsors and executives

lecture_notes.introduction: Different stakeholders need different information at different levels of detail. The PM must adapt communication to the audience.
key_concepts: Audience analysis: technical detail for the team, summary for executives, impact for end users
Influence without authority: reciprocity, consistency, social proof, liking, authority, scarcity
Managing up: providing sponsors with decision-ready information, no surprises
Difficult conversations: separating the person from the problem, focusing on interests not positions
Cultural communication differences in diverse teams
lecture_notes.demonstration: Presenting the same project problem to three audiences: team, sponsor, and end-user group

review.value: Give executives options with recommendations, not just problems; never let the sponsor be surprised
Build relationships, demonstrate expertise, and use reciprocity to gain cooperation from resources you don't control

learning_objectives: Identify sources of conflict in projects and apply resolution techniques
Apply principled negotiation from Getting to Yes
Select appropriate conflict resolution styles for different situations
readings: Fisher & Ury, Getting to Yes, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Conflict is inevitable in projects. The PM's job is not to avoid conflict but to manage it constructively.
key_concepts: Thomas-Kilmann modes: competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, accommodating
Principled negotiation: separate people from problems, focus on interests, generate options, use objective criteria
Common project conflicts: schedules, priorities, resources, technical approaches, personality
Escalation protocols: when and how to escalate unresolved conflicts
BATNA: Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play negotiation between a PM and a functional manager over shared resources

review.value: Win-win approach; preferred when the issue is important and the relationship matters; takes more time
Know your best alternative before negotiating; it defines your walk-away point and strengthens your position

learning_objectives: Select appropriate communication tools for different project needs
Manage communication in distributed and virtual team environments
Address communication barriers in cross-cultural projects
readings: Erin Meyer, The Culture Map, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern projects increasingly involve distributed teams across time zones and cultures. Technology enables but does not guarantee effective communication.
key_concepts: Tool selection: synchronous (video calls, chat) vs. asynchronous (email, wikis, shared docs)
Collaboration platforms: project management tools, document sharing, real-time messaging
Time zone management: overlapping hours, follow-the-sun handoffs, async-first culture
Cross-cultural communication: high-context vs. low-context cultures, direct vs. indirect feedback
Information security: classifying project information, secure communication channels
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a communication technology stack for a globally distributed project team across 4 time zones

review.value: Document decisions and context in writing so team members across time zones can catch up without waiting for meetings
High-context cultures (Japan, China) embed meaning in context; low-context cultures (US, Germany) state things explicitly

questions.question: A project team grows from 8 to 12 members. Calculate the number of communication channels before and after, and explain the management implication.
questions.answer: Before: 8(7)/2 = 28 channels. After: 12(11)/2 = 66 channels. Adding 4 people increased channels by 38 (a 136% increase). The management implication is that communication complexity grows much faster than team size, requiring more structured communication processes, clearer roles, and possibly sub-teams to manage information flow.

questions.question: Explain the difference between push, pull, and interactive communication, and give one project situation where each is most appropriate.
questions.answer: Push communication sends information to specific recipients without confirmation of understanding (e.g., sending a weekly status report email to stakeholders). Pull communication places information in a central location for recipients to access at their convenience (e.g., posting project documents on a shared drive or wiki for team reference). Interactive communication involves real-time exchange between parties (e.g., a problem-solving meeting to resolve a critical design issue requiring immediate team input).

questions.question: A project manager discovers a 3-week schedule delay. Describe how the PM should communicate this to: (a) the project team, (b) the project sponsor, and (c) the end-user community.
questions.answer: (a) Team: be transparent about the delay, explain the root cause, collaboratively discuss recovery options, assign action items for corrective measures. Use an interactive meeting format. (b) Sponsor: present the situation using BLUF format, quantify the impact (schedule, cost, scope), present 2-3 recovery options with pros/cons and a recommendation, request decisions on any needed changes. Use a formal briefing or memo. (c) End users: communicate the revised timeline, focus on how it affects them (delivery date change), explain what is being done to minimize impact, provide a point of contact for questions. Use formal written communication.

rubric.key: Appropriate end-user communication focusing on impact

questions.question: Two senior developers on your project disagree on the technical architecture. One wants microservices, the other wants a monolith. Using the Thomas-Kilmann model, which conflict resolution style should you use and why?
questions.answer: Collaboration is the best approach because both the technical decision and the working relationship are important. The PM should facilitate a session where both developers present their rationale with evidence (performance requirements, team skills, timeline constraints). Using principled negotiation, focus on interests (scalability, maintainability, delivery speed) rather than positions. The collaborative approach may yield a hybrid solution or help both parties agree on criteria for the decision. Compromise should be avoided here because a half-microservices/half-monolith architecture would be worse than either option.

rubric.key: Explains why other styles are less appropriate

major_assignments.title: Communication Management Plan and Stakeholder Communication Strategy
major_assignments.description: Develop a communication management plan for a project with at least 15 stakeholders across multiple groups, including a stakeholder communication matrix, meeting plan, and reporting framework.
objectives: Create a stakeholder communication matrix mapping information needs
Design a meeting cadence with agendas for each meeting type
Develop status report and dashboard templates
Address communication challenges for a distributed team
deliverables: Meeting plan with agendas for kickoff, status, and steering committee meetings
Status report template and dashboard mockup

criteria: Communication methods justified for each stakeholder group
All 15+ stakeholders mapped with information needs
Frequency, format, and method specified for each
Responsible parties identified for each communication
Meeting types clearly differentiated with purposes
Agendas include objectives, timeboxes, and pre-work
Meeting cadence is realistic and not excessive
Status report template is clear and actionable
Distributed team strategy addresses time zones and culture
Well-designed meeting plan with professional agendas

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on communication planning, techniques, and conflict resolution

communication_project.description: Communication management plan and stakeholder communication strategy

midterm_examination.description: Communication planning, written communication, and meeting management

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including stakeholder communication and conflict resolution

class_participation.description: Active participation in role-plays, facilitation exercises, and case discussions

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for role-play and facilitation exercises. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Role-play exercises are collaborative. The communication plan assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all frameworks and models referenced.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to collaboration tools (video conferencing, shared documents) and presentation software required.
